revision.text:
[[ja:愛子の田植踊]] '''Ayashi no Taue Odori''' (愛子の田植踊) is a {{ill|rice-planting dance|ja|田植踊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice-planting dance|qq=}}, a traditional folk performing art handed down in Ayashi, {{ill|Aoba-ku, Sendai|en|Aoba-ku, Sendai|qid=Q1042308|lt=Aoba-ku, Sendai|1=Aoba-ku, Sendai}}, {{ill|Sendai|en|Sendai|qid=Q46747|lt=Sendai|1=Sendai}}, Miyagi Prefecture. It is designated as a {{ill|Folk Cultural Property|lt=Folk Cultural Property|lt=Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財}} by Miyagi Prefecture[https://www.pref.miyagi.jp/site/sitei/02ayasi.html 県指定無形民俗文化財]（宮城県）. [[File:愛子の田植踊.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Ayashi no Taue Odori
Opening scene where Yajūrō delivers his introduction
(February 2016 / Sendai City Hirose Cultural Center)]] == Overview == The origins of the dance date back to the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|qid=Q1325001|lt=Genroku era|1=Genroku}} (1688–1704), attributed to a person named Katō who is said to have brought the tradition from Kyoto. However, its connection to the {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Shrine|qq=}} of Gotenyama in Ayashi and its hereditary association with Shugendō practices suggests a more localized development. Historically, the family managing the dance was called the "Niwa-moto" (庭元), serving as the gathering point for performers. From the lunar New Year until March 15, coinciding with the Suwa Shrine festival, performers would visit neighboring villages, dancing to pray for bountiful harvests and fostering community ties. Although it temporarily disappeared, the tradition was revived in 1930 (Shōwa 5) upon the opening of the {{ill|Senzan Line|en|Senzan Line|qid=Q1051995|lt=Senzan Line|1=Senzan Line}}. However, it declined again during wartime before being formally restored in 1950 (Shōwa 25) by a decision of the former {{ill|Hirose Village, Miyagi District|ja|広瀬村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Village, Miyagi District|1=Hirose Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q7545940}} council. Today, the Ayashi no Taue Odori Preservation Society conducts activities to preserve and pass down the tradition, with support from local elementary school social studies classes and a citizen supporter club based at the Sendai City Hirose Cultural Center. == Roles == The dance features the following roles: * Large Drum (Ōdaiko) * Small Drum (Shime-daiko) * Flute (Fue, tuned to the six-bamboo pitch) * Chant Leader (Uta-age) * Yajūrō * Bell Ringer (Suzufuri) * Saotome (rice-planting maidens) == Performance Contents == 1. Irēba 2. Hand Clapping (Tebayashi) 3. Bell Rhythm (Suzubyōshi) 4. Fan Rhythm (Ōgibyōshi) 5. Coin Drum (Zendaiko) 6. Jumping Drum (Tobidaiko) == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/site/sitei/02ayasi.html Ayashi no Taue Odori] (Designated Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property, April 23, 1960) * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/database/c00618.html Ayashi no Taue Odori] (Sendai City Designated/Registered Cultural Property) * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/manabu/bunkazai/shiryo/__icsFiles/afieldfile/2015/05/14/nenpou34.pdf Ayashi no Taue Odori] (Sendai City Cultural Property Survey Report No. 418, Cultural Property Yearbook 34, September 2013, Sendai City Board of Education) * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kOVjewFg52w Ayashi no Taue Odori Performance, Rekimin Autumn Festival 2012] (Dance by the Preservation Society) {{Cultural Properties of Japan|qq=}} {{japan-culture-stub|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|愛子の田植踊|version=71576224|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:愛子の田植踊]] {{wikidata link|Q24864987}} [[Category:Pre-celebration Performing Arts]] [[Category:Prefecturally designated intangible folk cultural property]] [[Category:青葉区 (仙台市)]]
